title: Ruined City Encounters
description: The bones of the old world — collapsed towers, flooded underpasses, overgrown plazas, and the dense vertical ruin of a city that used to hold millions. The rubble remembers. So does whatever's living in it.

entries.result: A building that is structurally sound on floors one through three and completely collapsed above — and someone has been living on the fourth floor somehow.
A flooded ground-floor corridor with clear, clean water, an uncertain depth, and sounds echoing from somewhere below.
A scavenger crew working systematically through an office building, floor by floor, who have reached floor six. Their communication is good and their security is watching the stairwells.
A pre-collapse mural, still vivid under the grime, depicting something that the people who lived here considered worth remembering.
A functioning elevator — power source unknown — in a building that has no other working systems.
A market operating in an atrium, informal and unannounced, that materializes here on specific days that locals know and travelers don't.
A gang checkpoint at a building entrance, collecting tolls to use the structure as a through-route between districts.
A pre-collapse parking structure used as stabling for animals, with a small community built around the ground-floor spaces.
A collapsed section of elevated highway that has become a bridge between two buildings, now a thoroughfare.
A rooftop garden, extensive, well-maintained, belonging to a community that has learned to stay off the ground.
A library, largely intact, with half its collection destroyed by water and the other half surprisingly readable. Someone has been organizing the survivors.
A street-level shop front that has become a clinic: red cross on the door, smells of antiseptic, queue outside.
A pre-collapse underground transit tunnel accessible through a collapsed station entrance, used as a travel route by those who know it.
A sniper position in a high window that covers the street below, currently unoccupied, with a clear line of sight to wherever the party is going.
A zone where the upper floors of two facing buildings have collapsed toward each other, creating an accidental enclosed space beneath.
A pre-collapse bank vault, open, cleaned out, now being used as a community safe room with the door repaired and a new lock.
A child's bedroom visible through an intact window on the third floor — still furnished, unchanged — in a building that's been structurally condemned.
A water tower, still holding water, with a community built around the base that controls access.
A pre-collapse fire station converted to a militia headquarters, with the apparatus bay used as a vehicle garage.
A section of the old city's financial district where the towers still stand, mostly intact but entirely empty, like a monument to the specific thing that ended.
A working pay phone — fully functional, unclear how — that sometimes connects and sometimes doesn't and no one has explained.
A community garden in a former plaza, irrigated by a clever system of rain collection and gravity, producing more than it has any right to.
A territorial boundary marked by painted lines on the street and by the understanding that crossing it without an invitation is a specific kind of statement.
A pre-collapse courthouse, intact, now used as a community meeting hall and de facto governance space by the surrounding district.
A basement room with working refrigeration — powered by a system no one currently living here installed — still cold.
A tunnel system under the city that connects several points, known to some factions and not others, with no consistent map.
A pre-collapse sports arena being used as a refugee camp, internally organized with its own economy and governance that outsiders are not automatically welcome in.
A building that appears empty from outside and is a community of forty-plus people inside, who have learned to maintain that appearance.
A pre-collapse hotel, high floors still inaccessible, lower floors colonized into a neighborhood with permanent residents and transients side by side.
A gang negotiation happening in a public space, both sides armed, watched by residents who know to stay back but can't entirely leave.
A section of city that was flooded and never drained, now navigable by boat in some channels, impassable on foot.
A pre-collapse hospital with a functional wing maintained by a small medical community who treat anyone who can reach them.
A street where every storefront has been converted to housing, the original purpose readable only through signs and fixtures.
A faction stronghold occupying an entire city block, fortified, self-sufficient, clearly long-established and clearly not interested in visitors.
A section of city under active excavation — survivors looking for specific pre-collapse infrastructure, organized, using equipment improvised from debris.
A pre-collapse police station used as a holding facility by a local power structure, not affiliated with any external authority.
An overgrown park at the center of a ruined block where the vegetation has grown so thick it's become navigational cover.
A community radio station broadcasting from the top floors of a still-standing tower, covering two districts and maintaining neutrality as deliberate policy.
A pre-collapse subway car, derailed in a tunnel collapse, now used as permanent housing by a family who has lived there for a decade.
A salvager who knows this district better than anyone, will guide for a price, and has opinions about which buildings are worth entering that are based on experience.
A building that is structurally compromised in a way that isn't obvious until weight is added to a specific floor — and the party is about to add weight to that floor.
A former city square where weekly trade happens, policed by a loose collective of neighborhood representatives with no formal authority and effective practical power.
A pre-collapse museum with most of its collection intact — protected by walls thick enough to survive what the surrounding buildings didn't — and a community that has made it a school.
A former luxury apartment building where the upper floors are still occupied by pre-collapse residents who have not left and have adapted in ways that are difficult to classify.
A gang that controls a specific underground access point and charges for its use, maintaining the infrastructure well enough to justify the toll.
A pre-collapse data center in a basement, still partially powered, with information no one has yet worked out how to extract.
A vertical neighborhood built between two standing towers, connected by improvised bridges and ladders, with its own internal logic of ownership and access.
A pre-collapse shopping mall, the exterior collapsed, the interior largely intact, now a self-contained community of several hundred people.
A former restaurant district where one establishment is still operating — pre-collapse recipes, improvised ingredients — and has a line out the door every day.
A section of city where every surface has been covered in survivor art — murals, carvings, written histories — creating a visual record of thirty years of post-collapse life.
A building with a working alarm system that triggers when anyone enters, connected to nothing, going off into an empty city.
A pre-collapse university building converted into a research center by survivors trying to preserve technical knowledge and extend it.
A blocked road where someone has been painstakingly clearing debris for months, alone, without help or apparent community support.
A faction checkpoint that has been abandoned — equipment left in place — in the last two to four hours. No sign of why.
A rooftop accessible by a route that takes knowledge to navigate, with a view of a significant portion of the city, used by whoever knows how to get there.
A pre-collapse infrastructure system — power, water, communications — that still partially works and that multiple factions are maneuvering to control.
A community that has rebuilt a specific block to pre-collapse standards: cleared, repaired, functioning utilities, maintained street — an island in the ruin.
A faction warehouse being loaded or unloaded, with armed guards and a supervisor who has questions about anyone nearby.
A pre-collapse cemetery, intact, being actively maintained by a community that has buried most of its dead here and considers it sacred ground.
A building fire — small, containable, but threatening to spread — with no organized response and residents doing what they can.
A pre-collapse transit hub with a community built around it, using the structure for cover, the tunnels for access, and the tracks as a controlled thoroughfare.
A scavenger competition — two crews working the same building from different sides, aware of each other, moving fast.
A survivor dispute being adjudicated publicly in a plaza, with the surrounding community as witness and informal jury, no formal law present.
A pre-collapse corporate headquarters, its signage still intact, now occupied by a community that has kept the name ironically.
A building with a population that has self-organized into something functionally indistinguishable from a pre-collapse neighborhood association, complete with dues and a complaint process.
A section of underground service tunnels still maintained and lit by a community that uses them as their primary transit between buildings.
A collapsed building being actively excavated by a community looking for a specific thing they know is in there.
A pre-collapse emergency broadcast tower still transmitting on a frequency no one currently alive set it to.
A neutral zone — declared and maintained by mutual agreement between three factions — where trade happens and violence is theoretically prohibited.
A building that is a functioning school, formal curriculum, attendance expected, operated by a community that has decided education is non-negotiable.
A street that floods in rain and drains in an hour through a system that someone has maintained since the collapse. Nobody knows who or how.
A rooftop antenna array, recently installed, transmitting something on a frequency that didn't exist before the collapse.
A pre-collapse government building, its records partially intact, that has become the factual basis for land and resource disputes across the district.
A water main that's been tapped and rerouted to serve a building that was never on the original system.
A faction moving a prisoner through the city with the quiet efficiency of people who have done this before and intend to do it again.
A pre-collapse theater with the stage still set, seats still arranged, and a community that has been performing original works there for fifteen years.
A building that is stable below the third floor and actively shifting above it, with residents who know which floors are safe and use them accordingly.
A dead faction member whose gear and markings suggest they were far outside their territory, on a route that makes no obvious tactical sense.
A former bank with a community credit system operating out of it, backed by tradeable goods, administered by a person who turns out to have been an economics teacher.
A community that has restored running water to their block through a gravity-fed system and is actively negotiating with other communities about access to it.
A section of ruined city that a faction has declared a demolition zone — they're removing structures deliberately — and the reason they're giving is not the reason they're doing it.
A pre-collapse zoo, most enclosures open, some animals still there, some having clearly been there the whole time.
A community that has found and reactivated a pre-collapse manufacturing system and is producing something the rest of the city wants.
A section of city that burned after the collapse and never recovered, now a field of rubble with things growing through it in organized rows. Someone planted this.
A pre-collapse power substation, still partially functional, that three factions are in quiet competition to control without appearing to.
A survivor who has been mapping the city's underground systems for six years and has found inconsistencies that suggest something is down there that wasn't on any pre-collapse plan.
A faction meeting in a neutral location — nervous, armed, professionally so — that has the character of a negotiation that could go wrong.
A community broadcasting pre-collapse media — music, film, audio — on a local frequency, operating as a pirate station, considered essential by its neighborhood.
A building with a functional greenhouse on the roof that has been producing food continuously for twenty years and that the building's community guards seriously.
A former public transit depot turned logistics hub, operated by a network that moves goods across the city on a schedule that works because everyone uses it.
A pre-collapse apartment building where each floor has become an independent community with different rules, different governance, and different relationships with the other floors.
A section of city where no factions operate and no community has established, but which sees constant foot traffic from people who don't make eye contact.
A pre-collapse data archive, mostly destroyed by fire, with one cabinet of records intact that contains information about pre-collapse infrastructure that would be very valuable to someone.
A street-level market in a former covered arcade, operating on barter, with a reputation for enforcing its own rules better than any faction in the district.
A community that has been in this building since the collapse — original inhabitants, their children, their grandchildren — with continuous oral history and a complicated relationship with newcomers.
A dead drop location — known to certain operators — currently active, with a package that was left this morning.
A faction stronghold that is undergoing a change in leadership, audible from outside as raised voices, and which will be different in some way by tomorrow.
A pre-collapse structure that was never fully documented and which contains something in a sealed section that everyone who knows it exists has decided not to open.
A community that has built something remarkable — remarkable enough to be worth protecting, worth traveling to see, worth fighting over — in the middle of the ruin.
The city breathes at night. Lights move between floors that should be empty. Signals pass between towers in patterns too regular for coincidence. Something in the ruin is organizing.
